observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. instruct the Municipal Assessor to assist the Municipal Engineer in the
assessment of the fair value of the road lot components of road network
account;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to recognize the assessment of the
Municipal Assessor and effect necessary adjustments in the book
balances of the road network account and compute separately the
depreciation for the road components based on their respective cost and
useful life;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide required disclosures in the
Notes to Financial Statements in the format required by the COA Circular
No. 2015-008;
d. instruct the General Service Officer (GSO) and Municipal Accountant to
prepare and maintain LRNPC and LRNPC, respectively, and ensure that
records are religiously reconciled; and
e. instruct the GSO to prepare and maintain The Local Road Inventory and
Road Map and reconcile it with those recorded in the Registry of Public
Infrastructure.

a. direct the Municipal Accountant to compute for the depreciation of the
PPE procured/completed in CY 2024 as detailed in Annex F of this report.
Furthermore, we recommend the accountant to record infrastructure
projects in the books at the time of completion and provide depreciation
for the period that these assets were already utilized;
b. require the Property Custodian to maintain an updated Property
Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) and that transfer of custody of assets
from one person to another should be properly documented.
Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant shall maintain an updated PPE
Ledger Cards to facilitate the accuracy and reliability of PPE accounts
and the Property Custodian shall ensure that all properties are tagged
with property stickers indicating the vital information required by rules
and regulations; and
c. Create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to fast track the appraisal
of the unserviceable properties totaling P6.9 million and dispose the
same to avoid further deterioration of the said assets in accordance with
Section 79 of P.D. No. No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry
to correct the overstatements in the Due from LGUs and Due to NGAs;
and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile
their records and effect necessary corrections. Furthermore, we
recommended the immediate submission to the audit team of the
liquidations for the fund transfers totaling P5.358 million.

a. direct the Municipal Assessor to submit to the audit team copies of OCT
and tax declaration of all land owned by the Municipality for verification
and conduct assessment of these properties to come up with the
revalued amounts;
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile with the Municipal Assessor
and adjust balances to reflect the revalued amounts of the properties
after the conduct of assessment; and
c. Acquire land title for the properties with fair market value totaling
₱7.035 million in compliance with Section 23 (2) Title I-B, Book, 1987 of the
Administrative Code.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. direct the Municipal Treasurer to request the transfer of the unexpended
DRRMF from previous years and current year totaling ₱2.777 million to the
depository account for Trust Liability-DRRM fund;
b. direct the Municipal Accountant effect the following adjusting entries to
reflect the correct balances of the accounts; and

c. henceforth, direct both the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to
comply with the regulations on the transfer of unexpended/unobligated
balance of the LDRRMF to the Trust Fund.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to intensify their
efforts to establish the existence and validity and likewise collect said
receivables, respectively; and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct an assessment of the
collectability of the dormant receivable accounts and request write-off
according to the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005
dated December 19, 2016 and henceforth, prepare and maintain
subsidiary ledgers for all receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of
the MNGAS for LGUs.

a. direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately the accumulated
undeposited collections totaling ₱176,085.63 and henceforth, ensure that
all collections should be deposited intact and in required frequency as
mandated by Item 33, Chapter 2 of the Revised Cash Examination Manual
to prevent the exposure of agency funds to possible misuse,
embezzlement, and/or risk of loss thru theft, robbery and the like.
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to henceforth prepare and submit to the
audit team quarterly reconciliation of the Cash in Local Treasury account
and effect the following adjusting entries to correct the balance of Cash
in Local Treasury accounts:
Account Debit Credit
General Fund
Cash in Local Treasury 1,085.57
Government Equity 1,085.57
To adjust error in recording of collections
Special Education Fund
Cash in Local Treasury 0.16
Government Equity 0.16
To recognize the overage incurred as of year-end
c. direct the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments and
reverse the amount of staled check to the corresponding Cash in Bank
and Accounts Payable accounts and henceforth, ensure the proper
monitoring of disbursements by check to prevent check payments from
becoming stale.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Commission on
Audit all financial reports and accounts within the time prescribed by
regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to reconcile the RPT and SET Receivable accounts with the list
of tax delinquencies maintained by the Municipal Treasurer and identify the
reason of the discrepancy and draw necessary adjusting entries to correct
misstatements in the financial statements, if any.

We recommended and Management agreed to demand from the concerned
barangays the immediate submission of the liquidation reports for the
outstanding fund transfers of ₱5.479 million through the issuance of follow-
up letters and/or demand letters. Moreover, if liquidation reports remain
unsubmitted after reasonable follow up and demands, we recommended
management to demand a refund from all concerned barangays in
compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. regulate the construction of buildings and other structures within the
Municipality for the safety of its constituents and generate additional
income by directing the Municipal Treasurer with the assistance of the
Municipal Engineer to impose the collection of building permit fees in
compliance with Sections 208 and 301 of the IRR of PD No. 1096.
b. direct the Municipal Treasurer to dispose the income to be generated
from the building permit fees and other charges in accordance with
DPWH and DILG JMC No. 001 dated July 4, 2013.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal
Engineer to ensure that DVs for payment for progress and final billings and
release of retention money for infrastructure projects are duly supported
with relevant documents prescribed in Item 9 of the COA Circular No.
2023-04 and ensure that no claims against the government funds shall be
processed and paid without complete documentation in adherence to the
P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the DTRs and ARs of
the job orders workers listed in the Annex H of this report and henceforth,
ensure that paid payrolls are duly supported with complete and properly
signed and accomplished supporting documents as required by pertinent
provisions.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. henceforth, submit list of all on-going government projects/ programs/
activities (PPAs) and those that were implemented at the beginning of the
year; and

b. instruct the Municipal Engineer to the audit team within ten days after the
award of the infrastructure projects or before the start of the PPAs that the
appropriate projects signboards and /or public notices are already posted at
the project site, for proper validation.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. ensure the proper operation and maintenance of the approved sanitary
landfill in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003 and ISWM
Plan; and

b. revisit the Integrated Solid Waste Management Plan covering only CYs
2015 to 2025 and make revisions accordingly to provide updated timeline
in the operation and implementation of said sanitary landfill site.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. Continue their compliance in allocating one percent of the total
appropriations and ensure the proper and quality implementation of all
PPAs identified to address issues and concerns of the elderly and PWDs
in the Municipality in compliance with DILG Memorandum Circular No.
2014-59; and

b. Instruct the focal person to ensure that Agency Implementation Report
related to the implementation of PPAs for the senior citizens and PWD
are prepared in the prescribed format and that said reports are
submitted by the end of January of the following year pursuant to DBM-
DSWD-NCSC-NCDA JMC No. 1 S. 2023.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inspectorate
Team/Committee to furnish the Office of the Auditor copies of IARs with the
corresponding POs within the prescribed period so that timely inspection
thereof can be conducted by the audit team.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit copies of duly
perfected contracts within five working days from perfection thereof to the
Auditor for prompt review and evaluation as required under COA Circular
No. 2009-001.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. instruct the BAC Secretariat to henceforth ensure the transparency and
accuracy of the bidding process by notifying in writing all bidders of the
bid opening details in compliance with Section 25.7 of the Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184;
b. ensure strict compliance with the prescribed timelines of the bidding
process to avoid delays in the implementation of projects; and

c. submit other supporting documents that were essential in the evaluation
during the bidding process and before the contracts were signed. Henceforth,
BAC ensure the strict implementation of the procurement process as
provided under the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to the GSIS the unremitted
balance of ₱267,481.91 immediately and henceforth, ensure that
withholdings of employees’ and employers’ contributions be remitted
intact and within the prescribed period of the RA No. 8291;
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit copies of the Alpha-Lists to
the audit team;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain schedules
and/subsidiary ledgers to validate the balances of Due to GSIS account
and submit the same to the audit team; and
d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that remittances of GSIS
withholdings are supported with Disbursement Vouchers and proof of
electronic payments to be attached with the Journal Entry Vouchers
submitted to the audit team.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to henceforth, ensure that withholdings of
employees’ and employers’ contributions be remitted intact and within the
prescribed period of the RA No. 9679;
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit copies of the Alpha-Lists to the
audit team;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain schedules
and/subsidiary ledgers to validate the balances of Due to Pag-IBIG
account and submit the same to the audit team; and
d. instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that remittances of Pag-IBIG
withholdings are supported with Disbursement Vouchers and proof of
electronic payments to be attached with the Journal Entry Vouchers
submitted to the audit team.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to the PHIC the unremitted
balance of ₱170,507.71 immediately and henceforth, ensure that
withholdings of employees’ and employers’ contributions be remitted
intact and within the prescribed period of the RA 10606;
b. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit copies of the Alpha-Lists to
the audit team;
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain schedules
and/subsidiary ledgers to validate the balances of Due to PhilHealth
account and submit the same to the audit team; and
d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that remittances of
PhilHealth withholdings are supported with Disbursement Vouchers
and proof of electronic payments to be attached with the Journal Entry
Vouchers submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation:
In order to establish accurately and reliably the balances of local road
networks and the related existence and condition thereof, we recommended
and Management agreed to undertake the following:

a. The Municipal Assessor assist the Municipal Engineer in the assessment
of the fair value of the road lot component of local roads under the
jurisdiction of the Municipal Government;
b. The Municipal Accountant recognize the amount pertaining to the road
lot as part of the recorded book value of the road network accounts.
c. The General Service Officer/Acting Municipal Treasurer to prepare and
maintain LRNLC and LRNPC as well as Local Road Inventory and
Local Road Map;
d. The Municipal Accountant maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and RPI and
conduct reconciliation with the GSO between the list of inventories per
local road inventory report and road map with those recorded in the RPI;
e. The Municipal Accountant to take up the book value of Road Networks
completed prior to CY 2015, if any; and
f. Municipal Accountant provide required disclosures in the Notes to
Financial Statements in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008.

In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended and Management
agreed to the following:

a. Management determine the details of the recorded Land in the books
amounting to ₱2.977 million;
b. The Municipal Assessor submit schedule of land owned by the
Municipality showing the site location and its corresponding values;
c. Management document the legality of ownership of the lots identified by
the Municipal Assessor through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in
the name of the LGU; and
d. The Municipal Accountant reconcile her records with the Municipal
Assessor and effect necessary entries to reflect the correct balance of
the Land account.

To reflect the correct balances of the accounts and to present fairly the
financial statements, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant
agreed that the necessary adjusting journal entries be prepared.

Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant take extra care in recording of
transactions following the prescribed Revised Chart of Accounts for Local
Government Units under COA Circular No. 2015-009.

In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended and Management
agreed to the following:

a. Management transfer the unexpended DRRMF from previous years and
current year totaling ₱365,290.90 to the depository account for Trust
Liability-DRRM fund;
b. The Municipal Accountant prepare necessary adjusting entries to reflect
the correct balances of the accounts; and
c. Henceforth, Management comply with the regulations on the transfer of
unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF to the Trust Fund.

We have reiterated our previous recommendation and the Municipal
Accountant agreed to look into the nature and composition of the dormant
receivables account of the LGU. In the event that composition of the said
accounts could no longer be determined, filing of a request for write off of
dormant receivables is in order pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008
(Guidelines on the Proper Disposition of Dormant Accounts).

We recommended and Management agreed to see to it that financial reports
and accounts are submitted in accordance within the time prescribed by
regulations for the timely post-audit of accounts and evaluation of current
financial condition and financial performance of the LGU.

In view of the above noted deficiencies, we recommended and Management
agreed to submit lacking documents listed in Item 10.5. Henceforth,
Management ensure that paid payrolls are duly supported with complete and
properly signed and accomplished supporting documents as required by
pertinent provisions.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to deposit collections
regularly and intact pursuant to above-mentioned rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to collect building
permit fees with the assistance of the Municipal Engineering Office and
dispose such fees in compliance with Section 208 of IRR of the National
Building Code of the Philippines and DPWH and DILG JMC No. 001 to ensure
the quality of buildings and other structures as well as the safety of its
constituents and to generate additional income to support LGU’s basic
services.

We recommended and Management agreed to revisit the LGUs Solid Waste
Management Plan and revise it accordingly to provide proper timeline of
implementation of various programs and projects, thus, see to it that
Sanitary Landfill be established in compliance with the above provisions.

We recommended and Management agreed to formulate plans, programs
and projects intended to address the needs of the senior citizens and
persons with disability, and integrate the same in their regular activities in
accordance with the Section 36 of the General Provisions of the FY 2023
General Appropriations Act.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to immediately remit

withheld as prescribed in Section 4 of Revenue Regulations 6-2001.

Moreover, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
prepare the necessary adjusting entry under the GF to reflect the correct
balances of the accounts.

11.4. Communication letters inviting observers were not duly received and
observers were not invited to attend in some of the procurement activity
in violation of Section 13 of the IRR of RA 9184; thus, there was no full
transparency in the procurement process.
11.4.1. It has been noted that communication letters inviting observers were
not duly received and observers were not invited to attend in some of
the stages of procurement for the above projects in violation of
Section 13 of the IRR of RA 9184 thus transparency in the
procurement process might be questioned.
11.4.2. Section 13 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184
provides among others the general guideline with regards to
observers in the bid opening process as follows: Section 13 of the
same IRR provides that to enhance the transparency of the
procurement process, the BAC shall, during the eligibility checking,
shortlisting, pre bid conference, preliminary examination of bids ,
bid evaluation and post qualification, invite, in addition to the
representation of COA, at least two (2) observers, with least one (1)
from a duly recognized private group in a sector or discipline
relevant to the procurement at hand and the other from a non-
government organization (NGO).
11.4.3. Inviting observers to attend the bid opening process promotes
transparency and ensures that the bidding is above board. Though it
is the sole prerogative/discretion of the members of the BAC to
recommend to the Local Chief Executive the winning bidder, the
state recognizes the fact that errors might occur during the actual
bidding process thus it required a certain number of observers to
attend the Bid opening whose sole function/ responsibility is to
assess the extent of compliance of the BAC members as to the
proper conduct of the public bidding process.
11.4.4. We have recommended that the BAC see to it that invitations are
sent to various observers five days before the conduct of the
eligibility checking, shortlisting, pre-bid conference, preliminary
examination of bids, bid evaluation and post qualification. Said
invitations should be duly received and properly filed and
attached together with the contracts submitted so that the team
will be able to determine management’s compliance with the said
promulgation.

11.5. Pre-bid conferences were conducted in less than twelve calendar days
before the deadline for the submission and opening of bids
11.5.1. Section 22.2 of the Revised I.R.R. of R.A. No. 9184 provides the
following regulation with regards to the conduct of pre-procurement
conference:
“The pre-bid conference shall be held at least twelve (12)
calendar days before the deadline for the submission and
receipt of bids..."
11.5.2. Based on the Procurement Manual of LGU for infrastructure projects
Volume II, the time period between the pre bid conference and bid
opening is mandatory and therefore cannot be shortened under any
circumstances in order to allow prospective bidders sufficient time to
examine, study and scrutinize all the bidding documents.
11.5.3. Said ruling had always been upheld and were always reiterated by
the GPPB. In its recent Non-Policy Opinion No. 46-2015, the latter
again clarified that deadline for the opening of bids should be shorter
than 12 days after the pre-procurement conference.
11.5.4. Contrary however to the said provisions, the audit team noted that
the deadline for the submission of bids for three procurements were
shorter than the prescribed 12 days between the pre-bid conference
and bid opening. Analysis is as follows:

11.6. Contractor’s All Risk Insurance were not duly provided for five
infrastructure projects
11.6.1. Clause 15.1 and 2 of the General Conditions of the Contract (GCC)
of the Philippine Bidding Documents, provides the requirement of
submission of Contractor‟s All Risk Insurance (CARI) as follows:
15.1. The Contractor shall, under his name and at his own
expense, obtain and maintain, for the duration of this
Contract, the following insurance coverage:
(a) Contractor’s All Risk Insurance;
15.2. The Contractor shall provide evidence to the Procuring
Entity’s Representative that the insurances required
under this Contract have been effected and shall, within
a reasonable time, provide copies of the insurance
policies to the Procuring Entity’s Representative. Such
evidence and such policies shall be provided to the
Procuring Entity’s through the Procuring Entity’s
Representative.
11.6.2. It has been noted that out of the 17 infrastructure projects subjected
for contracts review five failed to comply with the above provision.
Details are as follows:

11.6.3. As cleared by GPPB NPM 004-2006, the main responsibility of
preparing the bidding documents lies within the procuring entity;
hence, the procuring entity has the discretion to determine what to
include or what to require as additional documents needed for the
project with the condition that the minimum requirements as
contained in the ITB and GCC remained unchanged.
11.6.4. Since Clause 15.1 and 2 of the GCC specifically required all
contractors to post the CARI and that the premium of said insurance
is deemed incorporated in the Indirect Costs of the ABC, the BAC
should have required the submission of the CARI.
11.6.5. The CARI, in contrast with performance security, ensures that the
contractor‟s risks are properly covered and readily enforceable in
case the contractor rendered financially incapable to respond to all of
its liabilities in the contract.
11.6.6. We have recommended that BAC require all contractors to
obtain and submit the CARI.

11.7. Technical Requirements were not carefully reviewed by the BAC; hence,
contracts were awarded to contractors with doubtful qualifications.
11.7.1. Section 34 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 provides that:
The Lowest Calculated Bid/ Highest Rate Bid shall undergo
post-qualification in order to determine whether the bidder
concerned complies with and is responsive to all
requirements and conditions as specified in the Bidding
Documents.
11.7.2. Post-qualification is an integral part of the bidding process. It aims to
ascertain the validity and genuineness of the eligibility documents as
well as it determines the veracity of the claims made by the bidders
in both of their financial and technical proposals. Without
undergoing post qualification process, the BAC would not be able to
declare a bidder as the “lowest calculated and responsive bid”.
11.7.3. Post Qualification Evaluation Report as submitted by the BAC
indicated no adverse finding against the contractor; hence,
subsequently the award of contract has been made.

11.7.6. However, the BAC and TWG failed to call the attention of the
contractor or disclosed in their post-qualification evaluation report
the noted deficiencies in the submitted document, hence casting
doubt as to whether the TWG as well as the BAC members
conducted actual evaluation of the contractor‟s documents.
11.7.7. We have recommended that BAC should thoroughly
review/evaluate the submitted requirements of contractors.

11.8. Construction Safety and Health Program duly signed or approved by the
Department of Labor and Employment were not submitted by the
contractors.
11.8.1. Review of the submitted contracts disclosed that DOLE approved
Construction Safety and Health Program was not submitted by the
contractors prior to the contract signing.
11.8.2. Section 37.2.3 of the same IRR provides among others the following
contract documents to be submitted before the contract signing:
h. Other contract documents that may be required by existing
laws and/or the Procuring Entity concerned in the
Bidding Documents, such as the construction schedule
and S-curve, manpower schedule, construction methods,
equipment utilization schedule, construction safety and
health program approved by the Department of Labor
and Employment, and PERT/CPM or other acceptable
tools of project scheduling for infrastructure projects.
11.8.3. Item 5.2 of the LGU procurement manual provides the additional
technical requirement needed to be submitted by the contractor:
Construction Safety and Health program of the contractor
(This refers to a narrative description of the safety and health
program of the contractor in accordance with DOLE D.O.
No. 13. This program shall then be submitted by the winning
bidder to the Bureau of Working Conditions of DOLE before
any projects may be allowed to start).
11.8.4. Item G.3.A. of the procedural guidelines for DOLE Department
Order No. 13 also provides the following:
No construction schedule shall be undertaken without a
DOLE approved Construction Safety and Health program
and the required minimum personnel complement to
implement the same.
11.8.5. We have recommended that Management submit justification on
the noted deficiency. Henceforth, the BAC should require the
contractor to submit a DOLE approved Construction Safety and
Health Program prior to the contract signing. Failure to comply
on the part of shall cause disqualification of the contractor.

11.9. Every page of the submitted contract documents does not bear the initial
of the BAC members or their duly authorized representatives
11.9.1. Section 29 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 provides the
following guidelines on the opening of bids:
The BAC shall adopt a procedure for ensuring the integrity,
security and confidentiality of all submitted bids...
11.9.2. Whereas, Item 5.7 of the Procurement Manual for Local Government
Units Vol III provides the procedure needed to comply with the
above cited provisions, to wit:
All members of the BAC or their duly authorized
representatives who are present during the bid opening,
shall initial every page of the original copies of all bids
received and opened.”
11.9.3. It is to be noted however, that said internal control measure is yet to
be adopted by the LGU. All contracts submitted for CY 2020 had not
complied with the said requirements; hence, the audit team was
precluded in determining whether the said documents were present
during the bid opening.
11.9.4. We have recommended that the BAC see to it that after verifying
the documents which would form part of the contract, the BAC
properly affix their signature so as to ensure that no alterations
will be made upon the submitted documents.

11.10. Posting requirements of Notices of award and Notice to Proceed in the
LGU‟s PhilGEPS were not adhered to; hence, campaign of the
government for transparency, accountability and participation was not
effectively pursued.
11.10.1. In line with the mandate of every government agency to fully utilize
PhilGEPS in every phase of procurement, Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2
of the Implementing Rules and Regulations provided that:
37.1 Contract Award
37.1.6 The BAC, through the Secretariat, shall post,
within three (3) calendar days from its
issuance, the Notice of Award in the
PhilGEPS, the website of the Procuring
Entity, if any, and any conspicuous place in
the premises of the Procuring Entity.
37.4 Notice to Proceed
37.4.2 The Procuring Entity, through the BAC
Secretariat, shall post a copy of the Notice
to Proceed and the approved contract in the
PhilGEPS and the website of the Procuring
Entity, if any, within fifteen (15) calendar
days from the issuance of the Notice to
Proceed.
11.10.2. For the year under audit, we commend the management‟s earnest
approach in adhering to the posting requirements for the initial
bidding process activities, however, we also noted that the LGU had
not posted the notices of award and proceed for the procurement of
goods and infrastructures on projects undertaken through
competitive bidding or alternative methods.
11.10.3. It is to be reminded that under the provisions of the IRR of R.A. No.
9184 it mandated all procuring entity to fully use the PhilGEPS. Said
provision, however, should not be confined up to registration and
initial activities before the bidding process only. Such provision also
includes the posting of the Notice of Award (NOA) and Notice to
Proceed (NTP) in the PhilGEPS which the Municipal Government
failed to comply.
11.10.4. We have recommended that the Management adhere to the
transparency provisions as provided by the regulations cited
above.

12.5.8. In view of the above noted lapses, we have recommended the
following:
(a) The Municipal Mayor convenes the SWMB and seeks
justification as to the reason for the low success rate for the
implementation of SWM projects;
(b) The members of the SWMB revisit the provisions of the IRR
of R.A. No. 9003. Henceforth, implement quality assurance/
monitoring mechanism to facilitate an efficient and effective
implementation of the programs indicated in the SWMP

13.9.7. We have recommended that Management submit the lacking
documents and ensure that the required documents for
procurements through negotiated procurement (emergency
cases) are complete before payments are made in accordance
with the provisions of the GPPB Circular No. 01-2020.

13.10.7. We have recommended that management henceforth prepare
Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances
and furnish the same to the NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy
furnished the Audit Team within ten working days after the end
of each quarter pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and
2020-009.

14.7. We have recommended that Management evaluates the unimplemented
PPA whether these are still priority concerns and implement or re-
appropriate them accordingly.

16.3. We have recommended that the management provide in the Notes to
Financial Statements all the required disclosures as mandated under
COA Circular No. 2014-002.

17.5. We have recommended that management see to it that GAD activities are
identified through the use of HGDG and that the GAD plan are regularly
updated and that the identified projects address identified GAD issues
and concerns.

18.6. In view of the noted deficiencies, we have recommended that the
MSWDO, being the leading authority in identifying gender issues and
concerns:
18.6.1. Initiate the strengthening of the Gender and Development Inter-
Agency Committee by solidification of its Gender Responsive
Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) System to execute proper
determination of GAD objectives and accomplishment of
projects; and
18.6.2. Accompany GAD ARs with the required reports/documents be
furnished to COA to facilitate audit of GAD Fund utilization.

observations.recommendation:
1.6. We recommended that, in order to establish accurately and reliably the
balances of local road networks and the related existence and condition
thereof, the management undertake the following:
1.6.1. The Municipal Engineer and the Municipal General Services
Officer conduct a yearly physical count of local road network
projects and consequently update the local road map of the
LGU;
1.6.2. The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal General Services Officer the description and cost
segregation of road components of various road infrastructure
projects;
1.6.3. The Municipal Accountant to keep and maintain subsidiary
leger for roads and its components and reconcile the same result
of the physical inventory at the end of each year. Also, provide
for the depreciation for the local road network and disclosure in
the Notes to Financial Statements; and
1.6.4. The Municipal General Services Officer maintain a Local Road
Inventory and Road Map and keep a complete Local Road
Network Property Cards for all roads and its components.

2.6. We recommended that in order to establish accurately and reliably the
balances of PPE accounts and forestall any losses, determine the actual
existence and condition of properties, the Municipal Mayor form a
committee to conduct inventory taking and reconcile discrepancies noted.
Moreover, result of the inventory should be submitted to the Audit Team
through the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) for
verification.

3.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should look into the
nature and composition of the dormant receivables account of the LGU.
In the event that composition of the said accounts could no longer be
determined, filing of a request for write off of dormant receivables is in
order.

4.5. We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare the Certified List of Taxpayers and submit a copy to the
Municipal Accountant as basis in setting up the receivables and deferred
income in order to present fairly the financial statements.

5.6. We reiterate our previous recommendation that the Municipal
Accountant analyze the entries made on RPT/SET Receivables and their
corresponding reciprocal accounts for transactions prior to CY 2018 to
determine the causes of the discrepancies and effect necessary
adjustments for fair presentation in the financial statements.

6.6. We recommended that Municipal Accountant see to it that the budgeted
amounts in the SCBAA match with the approved budget. Moreover, the
actual amounts in the SCBAA should reflect the actual expenditures and
are properly classified as to MOOE or CO.

7.7. We recommended that management comply with the regulations on the
transfer of unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF to the Trust
Fund. The municipal accountant likewise see to it that
unexpended/unobligated funds from LDRRM are treated as Trust
Liability at the end of the year and duly transferred to a special trust
fund account.

8.12. We recommended the following:
8.12.1. the management issue demand letters to the concerned
Barangays to submit their corresponding liquidations of fund
transfers;
8.12.2. the Municipal Accountant submit the Journal Entry Voucher
together with the supporting documents to validate recorded
liquidations amounting to ₱139,814.40; and
8.12.3. the Municipal Accountant should monitor the fund transfers
made to Barangays, maintain subsidiary ledgers, see to it that
required documents are complete and refrain from granting
additional funds to barangays with outstanding fund transfers.

9.7. In view of the noted discrepancies between the records of the Municipal
Government and other government agencies, we recommended that the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer:
9.7.1. reconcile their record with the GSIS, PAG-IBIG and
PHILHEALTH; and
9.7.2. regularly reconcile their quarterly financial records with the
records of other government agencies.

10.10. We recommended that management justify the noted deficiencies.
Henceforth, all cash advances should be supported with the required
documents such as estimate of expenses and approved payrolls and
strictly follow the guidelines set forth in COA Circular 97-002 in the
grant and liquidation of cash advances, Section 89 of PD 1445 and Annex
B of COA Circular 2012-03.

10.11. We recommended further the following corrective measures:
10.11.1. Limit the amount of cash advances for Payroll Fund to the net
amount of payrolls for a particular period only;
10.11.2. Set a fixed amount of cash advance for the payment of recurring
operating expenses only; and
10.11.3. Have a separate cash advance for Payroll Fund and another for
the payment of recurring operating expenses, and at no instance
should an operating expense be charged to the cash advance for
Payroll Fund.

11.6. We recommended that the management submit a written justification for
processing salaries, payrolls and other personnel benefits without duly
approved and complete documentation and for failure to submit the
required supporting documents. The Municipal Accountant should
immediately submit all the required documents needed to establish the
validity and legality of paid personnel services.

12.9. We recommended that fully accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets be
prepared to ensure proper use of government vehicle and consumption of
fuel, summary of which shall be prepared at the end of each month in a
Monthly Report of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption and
submit to this Office for audit/evaluation.

13.9. We recommended that Management -

submit to COA the lacking documents enumerated under Items
1.8.1 and 1.8.2for audit purposes and to support the validity of

and Work Accomplished prepared by the Municipal Engineer;
and
13.9.2. Henceforth, see to it that all disbursement vouchers are duly
documented pursuant to Section 4.6 of PD 1445 to establish
legality of payments. Attending payments/claims not properly
documented be discontinued.

to uncover any possible inferior construction materials used or
components supplied by the contractor so as to ensure the quality
of projects; and that the results be required before the payment of
the contractor’s accomplishments; and
14.6.2. Ensure that the provision on the conduct of material testing and
the submission of the test results as a requirement of final
payment be incorporated in the Special Condition of the Contract.

engineering activities before the conduct of any bidding.

review.

15.6.4. We recommended that the BAC conduct pre-bid conference on

15.7.8. We recommended that the BAC conduct pre-procurement
conference on all contracts with an approved budget of
₱5,000,000.00 or more for infrastructure and ₱2,000,000.00 or
more for goods and submit to the audit team the supporting

15.9.7. We recommended that management justify the above noted

15.10.5. We recommended that the BAC justify the noted deficiencies

15.11.5. We recommended that management submit justification on the

properly affix their signature so as to ensure that no alterations
will be made upon the submitted documents.

15.13.4. We recommended that the management justify the noted lapses

16.10. In view of the above noted deficiencies, we recommended the following:

submitted to the Audit Team for proper review and evaluation, and;

properly supported with complete documentation before payments
are made.

erring officials and employees.

said accounts and discrepancies between reciprocal accounts and effect
necessary adjustments for fair presentation in the financial statements.
Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should take extra care in recording
and posting of transactions involving reciprocal accounts.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare monthly
transmittal of submitted disbursement vouchers, official receipts, payrolls
and journal entry vouchers to the Municipal Accountant, who after
recording in the Books of Accounts, shall then submit the same to the
Auditor for the conduct of timely audit so as to facilitate timely
communication of noted deficiencies. This way, management could easily
pinpoint the persons responsible and determine the cause of delay of
submission of accounts and reports.

We have recommended that management ensure compliance with the
conduct of inventory and the recognition of Road Networks in the books of
accounts equivalent to the required transition period, as well as in the
maintenance of separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its component
and the disclosure thereof in the Notes to Financial Statements pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2015-008.

That the Disbursing Officer/Accountable Officer regularly submit his Report
of Disbursements / Liquidation documents to the Accounting Office for
timely recording in the books of accounts for fair presentation of the
financial statements. Likewise, he shall refund any unexpended balance of
his cash advance at the end of the year as prescribed under regulations;

That the Municipal Accountant require the Disbursing Officer/Accountable
Officer to submit promptly his liquidation documents and shall record the
liquidations on time to ensure that all transactions are included in the
financial statements, and record liquidation only when properly supported
with liquidation report and supporting documents; and

No cash advance should be granted to an accountable officer unless the
previous cash advance given to him has been fully liquidated or properly
accounted for.

We have recommended that all Memorandum of Agreements entered into by
the municipality, NADAI and SARO be submitted to the audit team for
audit/verification. Henceforth, ensure that trust funds received from
National Government Agencies and all other sources are covered by
Memorandum of Agreement/NADAI/SARO, and that the same shall be
submitted to COA within 5 days from perfection thereof for audit purposes.

The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer install effective
internal control on the municipality’s collection system including controls
for the issuances, reporting, monitoring and safeguard of all accountable
forms of the municipality;

The Municipal Treasurer properly verify the Reports of Collections and
Deposits of all collectors and monitor issuances and balances of
accountable forms. Check the Statement of Accountable Forms as to
initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending balances on hand
and make a physical count of accountable forms remaining in the custody
of the collectors and check against ending balances on hand column;

The Municipal Treasurer to issue new set of accountable forms to the
collectors only when the previously issued accountable forms are fully
issued and properly reported in the RCDs;

The Municipal Treasurer to present all the unaccounted accountable
forms and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor for verification.

Valid justifications be submitted to explain the deficiencies noted on the
unaccounted/missing accountable forms, and the delayed reporting of
collections/late remittances.

We have recommended that management explain the above-noted lapses.
Henceforth, all procurements of the municipality should be conducted
through public bidding as mandated under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, unless
justified by the conditions provided under Section 52 of the IRR as
recommended by the BAC and duly approved by the head of the procuring
entity. Alternative methods of procurement shall be resorted to only in
highly exceptional cases provided for in R.A. No. 9184. In all instances, the
procuring entity shall ensure that the most advantageous price for the LGU
is obtained.

We have recommended that Management require the concerned personnel to
submit the required supporting documents COA for audit purposes and
direct the concerned department head/personnel to submit justification for
the noted lapses. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant see to it that
succeeding transactions be properly supported with complete documentation
and in accordance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in order to
establish the validity and propriety of the transactions and disbursements.
Processing and payment of claims that are not properly documented shall be
discontinued.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the personnel in-
charge with release of contracts and bidding documents to furnish the
auditor with copies of these documents together with the required
attachments/supporting papers within five (5) days after perfection thereof
as required by COA Circular No. 2009-001 for auditorial and technical
review.

We have recommended that the municipality utilize the unexpended balance
of allocated funds for the intended purposes after due evaluation whether
these are still priority concerns taking into consideration current
developments and to accordingly re-appropriate the funds of PPA which
have become irrelevant or not responsive to current priority undertakings.

We have recommended that management justify the noted lapses.
Henceforth, ensure the existence of an appropriation in accordance with
Section 7 of R.A. No. 9184, Section 85(1) of P.D. No. 1445, Section 305 of R.A.
No. 7160 and the afore-cited jurisprudence, before transactions are made or
contracts are entered into by the municipality. Fund availability is, as it has
always been, an indispensable prerequisite to the execution of any
government contract involving the expenditure of public funds by all
government agencies at all levels. Also, the municipality strictly comply with
the provisions of R.A. No. 9184 particularly on the bid prices that exceed this
ceiling shall be disqualified outright.

17.1.3 We have recommended that management submit monthly Report on
the Receipt and Utilization of the DRRMF sourced from GAA and
Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations of the
municipality to NDRRMC through the OCD on or before the 5th day
following the end of each month, and furnish copy to the audit team
for audit/evaluation.

17.2 We have recommended that management sees to it that in cases of lump sum
appropriations containing general terms, a separate SB appropriation
ordinance/ resolution must always be secured by the Municipal Mayor to serve as an authority to enter into
contract/transaction in behalf of the Municipal Government in
accordance to Section 22(c) of R.A. No. 7160 and the applicable
jurisprudence.

17.3 We have recommended that the Municipal Government utilize funds
for the specific purpose it was created for in consonance with Section
336 of R.A. No. 7160 to prevent charges of technical malversation.

17.4 We have recommended that management ensure the existence of an
appropriation in accordance with Section 7 of R.A. No. 9184, Section
85(1) of P.D. No. 1445, Section 305 of R.A. No. 7160 and the afore-
cited jurisprudence, before transactions are made or contracts are
entered into by the municipality. Fund availability is, as it has always
been, an indispensable prerequisite to the execution of any
government contract involving the expenditure of public funds by all
government agencies at all levels.

17.5 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up
purchase of inventory under the appropriate Inventory account and
be only taken up under the appropriate Expense account upon
distribution to end-users/beneficiaries properly supported with RIS
and RSMI. We have also recommended for the submission of the
listings of recipients/beneficiaries (with their address and contact
number, if any, for audit purposes) duly signed acknowledging receipt
showing the items and quantity of items actually received.

17.6 We have recommended that management comply with the regulations
on the transfer of unexpended/ unobligated balance of the LDRRMF
to the Trust Fund.

17.7 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose all the
necessary information pertaining to LDRRMF. Henceforth, strictly
comply with the disclosure requirements on LDRRMF as provided by
COA Circular No. 2014-002.

We have reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal
Accountant take up the proposed adjusting entry for the unrecorded taxes
withheld in CY 2014, and make the necessary adjusting entries for CY 2016.
Hence, take extra-care in recording transactions. We have also
recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer
remit the corresponding internal revenue taxes as mandated by laws, rules
and regulations on all its purchases of goods and payments for services, and
income tax withholdings for additional income of the government and to
avoid possible imposition of sanctions by the BIR.

We have recommended that Management should invite a representative of the agency auditor to serve as witness in the conduct of the physical count of PPE to be conducted on or before December 31 of each year. The RPCPPE should be submitted to the Auditor on or before January 31 of each year for Audit audit/evaluation purposes. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer should see to it that all PPE were properly supported with updated ARE/MR. AREs/MRs should be renewed every three years. If not, these should not be considered in the physical count of PPE in accordance with Section 492 of GAAM Vol. 1.
observations.managementAction: pg 34, AAR 2015 invited COA representative as witness in the conduct of the annual physical count of PPE. In addition, CY 2016, and physical count of equipment of the Municipality submitted Report of Physical Count of PPE. Further, ARE/MR, PPE Ledger Cards per category of PPE of both the Accounting and Property Units, were not yet provided to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant should take up in the books of accounts the under-recordings and that the Municipal Treasurer should remit immediately the amount due to the BIR henceforth strictly comply on prompt remittance of taxes withheld. The LGU should exert effort to help the BIR in its collection of taxes in as much as it is entirely dependent on its share from IRA. The erring municipal officials concerned should be identified to answer personally the imposition and payment of the penalties, interest and surcharges.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the LGU’s budget allocation should be at least 5% of the total budget appropriations as authorized in the GAA to address the gender issues and concerns of the municipality. We have recommended that the GAD plan and accomplishment reports should contain the specific programs, projects and activities for proper monitoring and evaluation of GAD implementation. GAD focal persons should prepare the annual and performance-based LGU GAD Plan and Budget (LGPB) in response to the gender issues and concerns of the municipality and in the context of the LGU mandate. Expenditures should be limited to the specific PPAs identified in the annual and performance-based LGPB for full attainment of the purpose of the fund. Also, expenses made should not exceed the appropriated amount for every PPAs.
observations.managementAction: pg 69, AAR 2014 GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2016 was not supported with DILG-approved GAD Plan and the GAD Accomplishment Report was submitted late for the audit.
